question_title: Will SpaceX launch Starship Flight 13 before July 12, 2026?
summary: Flight 13 must lift off before July 12, 2026 UTC — an ~11-day window from now. Two binding constraints are both unsatisfied: (1) the FAA Flight 12 mishap investigation has no confirmed closure or new launch license (declared May 27; no return-to-flight statement found), and (2) hardware is not flight-ready — Ship 40 performed only a single-engine static fire on June 29, with a full static fire, stacking, and likely a wet dress rehearsal still pending. Even SpaceX's fastest historical return-to-flight (~2.5 months after a mishap) exceeds the available window. Trackers list NET July with some as late as July 31; the provisional ~July 3 target is explicitly estimated and stale. SpaceX's aggressive parallel campaign and quick self-led investigations provide some upside, but YES requires nearly everything to break right simultaneously. Estimate ~9%.

factors.description: FAA Flight 12 mishap investigation not confirmed closed; no FAA-approved license/date as of late June — a hard gate to any launch
Hardware not flight-ready: Ship 40 only did a single-engine static fire June 29; full static fire, stacking, likely WDR still pending before July 11
Launch trackers list NET July, some as late as July 31; provisional July 3 explicitly estimated/pending FAA
SpaceX aggressive parallel campaign (Booster 20 has 33 engines + cryo done) and self-led investigations can close fast
Starship's strong track record of slipping past provisional launch targets

reasoning:
**Forecast: 9% (NO likely)**

Will SpaceX launch Starship Flight 13 before July 12, 2026? **~9%**

The ~11-day window faces two unsatisfied hard gates: an open FAA mishap investigation from Flight 12 (declared May 27, no confirmed closure/license) and incomplete hardware preparation (Ship 40 only did a single-engine static fire on June 29, with full static fire, stacking, and likely a wet dress rehearsal still ahead). Even SpaceX's fastest historical return-to-flight exceeds the available window, and trackers center on later July. A YES would require nearly everything to break right at once — possible given SpaceX's aggressive cadence, but unlikely.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if SpaceX conducts an integrated test flight of Starship designated as Flight 13 (the 13th full-stack Starship/Super Heavy launch attempt) with liftoff occurring at any time before July 12, 2026 (UTC). "Liftoff" means the vehicle clears the launch mount under its own power; an attempt that ignites engines but aborts on the pad without leaving the mount does NOT count. The flight does not need to be successful. Resolution will be based on the "List of Starship launches" Wikipedia page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Starship_launches), which records a dated Flight 13 entry, or on [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting the launch. If no Flight 13 liftoff has occurred before July 12, 2026, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: "Flight 13" refers to the next integrated/orbital-class Starship test flight following Flight 12 (launched May 22, 2026), regardless of the specific booster or ship serial numbers used. If SpaceX renames or renumbers the flight, the next full-stack integrated launch attempt after Flight 12 is what counts. Static fires, cryogenic tests, single-stage tests, and other ground or non-integrated activities do not count. The flight's success or failure has no bearing on resolution; only whether liftoff (clearing the mount) occurs before July 12, 2026 (UTC). If the Wikipedia page is unavailable or has not yet been updated, resolution will rely on credible reporting of the launch.

factors.description: FAA mishap investigation from Flight 12 not confirmed closed; no FAA-approved license/date as of late June. Hard gate to launch.
Hardware not flight-ready: Ship 40 did only single-engine static fire June 29; full static fire, stacking, possible WDR still pending before July 11
Trackers list NET July, some as late as July 31; provisional July 3 explicitly estimated/pending
SpaceX aggressive parallel campaign, Booster 20 + Ship 40 both far along, self-led investigation can close quickly
Starship strong history of date slippage past provisional targets

input.anchor: Base rate ~15% for launch in an ~11-day window when hardware is mid-integration and a regulatory mishap gate is open
input.assessment: Question: liftoff of Flight 13 before July 12 UTC — ~11 day window. Two binding constraints both unsatisfied: (1) FAA mishap investigation closure/license not confirmed; (2) hardware only at single-engine static fire stage on June 29, needing full static fire + stacking + likely WDR. Even SpaceX's fastest path from this state typically takes ~2+ weeks. Provisional July 3 target is clearly stale/optimistic; trackers center on later July. YES requires everything to break right simultaneously. Low probability.
input.calibration_notes: Status quo (no launch) strongly favored. Not hedging to 50% — evidence clearly points NO. Resisted overconfidence (kept ~9% not 3%) because SpaceX can move fast and a single-engine SF could be quickly followed by full SF + rapid stack.
input.tool_audit: web_search returned rich, current results (June 29 static fire, FAA mishap status). No failures. Did not need Wikipedia fetch given strong news coverage.
input.process_reflection: Straightforward time-to-event question; web_search was sufficient. The main signal (single-engine static fire June 29 + open mishap investigation) directly bounds the timeline. No friction.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I verified each factor against the research trace and the worldview store, and found the forecast well-grounded.

FACTOR CHECKS:
1. FAA mishap not closed (logit -1.6): Grounded. Trace confirms FAA declared mishap May 27, 2026; the "investigation closed/return-to-flight" query returned only the grounding order plus an unrelated 2023 closure — no Flight-12 closure. Worldview research (faa-starship-mishap... entry, state=fresh) corroborates "still open as of June 5" and adds precedent RTF durations (Flight 8→9 ~2.5 months; IFT-1 ~5 months). Sign correct.
2. Hardware not flight-ready (logit -1.3): Grounded. Tesla Oracle June 29 article in trace: "single-engine static fire test on Starship 40... no final date yet." NASASpaceFlight confirms both vehicles only at static-fire stage, not yet stacked.
3. Trackers NET July / July 31 (logit -0.7): Core claim grounded — rocketlaunch.org "Last Update: NET July," Space Launch Live "Jul 31, 2026," multiple "July 2026" entries. The specific "provisional July 3" date is NOT clearly visible in the trace results; minor unsupported detail, but the underlying conclusion (trackers center on July/later) is well-grounded. Not forecast-changing.
4. Aggressive parallel campaign (logit +0.7): Grounded — Booster 20 has 33 engines installed + completed cryo; parallel campaigns documented. Positive sign correct.
5. History of slippage (logit -0.6): Reasonable base-rate factor, consistent with documented Starship history.

NO double-counting, NO wrong-direction factors, NO hallucinated core claims. Resolution criteria were PROVIDED and the agent engaged correctly with the "liftoff clears mount before July 12 UTC" definition and the Wikipedia source. No pre-publication event (Flight 13 hasn't flown). No regime-window issue (binary, no Monte Carlo).

PROBABILITY: My independent estimate is 8-12%, matching the agent's 9%. Two hard gates (open FAA mishap; hardware only at single-engine static fire on June 29, needing full static fire + stacking + likely WDR) must both resolve favorably within ~11 days. Even SpaceX's fastest historical RTF (~2.5 months for Flight 8→9) exceeds the available window from the May 27 mishap. The 9% sits appropriately below the 15% anchor; the departure is justified by the FAA gate, not narrative accumulation.

COUNTERARGUMENT: Strong — constructs a coherent alternative (timeline compression + fast FAA return-to-flight determination + smooth full static fire) with specific mechanisms, not a token gesture. Confidence 0.80 is calibrated to the solid, current evidence.

MINOR (non-blocking): (a) the "provisional July 3" date isn't visible in the trace; (b) tool_audit says "did not need Wikipedia fetch" but metrics show 1 wikipedia call (the Flight 12 page surfaced as search api_data) — immaterial. None affect the forecast direction or magnitude.
